title: Declaration on Building Equitable Societies Through Education
text:
WE, THE MINISTERS AND
REPRESENTATIVES OF
 Australia, Austria, Belgium, Bulgaria, Canada,
Chile, Colombia, Costa Rica, Croatia, the Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia,
Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Iceland, Ireland, Israel, Italy,
Japan, Korea, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Mexico, the Netherlands, New
Zealand, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Romania, the Slovak Republic, Slovenia,
Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, Türkiye, the United Kingdom, the United States, and
the European Union, met at the OECD headquarters in Paris on 7-8 December 2022
for the meeting of the Education Policy Committee (EDPC) at Ministerial level,
co-chaired by Korea, Norway and Portugal, supported by Estonia, Finland, Greece
and Japan, as Vice-Chairs to discuss how education can build the foundations
for accessible, equitable, inclusive, sustainable democratic societies and how
this can be enhanced through co-operation in the OECD framework.

WE CONDEMN
 Russia’s
unjustifiable, unprovoked and illegal war of aggression against Ukraine in the
strongest possible terms as a flagrant violation of international law that
shakes the very foundation of the international order, in line with the 2022
OECD Ministerial Council Statement; 
RECALL
 the decision of the OECD
Council of 8 March 2022 to immediately suspend participation of the Russian
Federation and Belarus in OECD bodies; and 
EXPRESS OUR DEEP CONCERNS

about the far-reaching consequences of the ongoing war in Ukraine, which has
caused the deaths of children, and destroyed schools and educational
facilities. It exacerbates an already-existing education crisis caused by the
pandemic that continues to cause irreparable damage to the emotional well-being
of children, learners, and educators, and has spillover effects on other
countries in the region and worldwide. 

WE STAND IN SOLIDARITY
 with the
people and the democratically elected government of Ukraine and 
HIGHLIGHT

the need to address the cognitive, social and emotional needs of all Ukrainian
learners affected by the war, in and outside of Ukraine. 

WE RECALL
 the OECD
60th Vision Statement “
Trust in Global
Co-operation – The Vision for the OECD for the Next Decade
”, which
reasserts our core values, reaffirms our founding goals and provides that the “
OECD
will continue its important work on […] research, education, lifelong learning
and skills
”.

WE RESTATE
 our
commitment to support the implementation of the Sustainable Development Goals
(SDGs), and, in particular, Sustainable Development Goal 4, which aims to “
ensure
inclusive and equitable quality education and promote lifelong learning
opportunities for all
”.

WE WELCOME
 the role of
the OECD to support Member and partner countries in their efforts to achieve
high-quality lifelong learning for all, which contributes to personal
development, fair and sustainable economic growth, social cohesion and
well-being. 

WE WELCOME
, in this
context, the continuous co-operation between the OECD and other international
organisations active in this area. 

WE RECOGNISE
 that OECD
education benchmarks and analysis have become global references for education
and help respond to the challenges of a changing world and the necessity to
develop relevant new competencies.

WE RECOGNISE
 that
responsibility for education within countries may be held at different levels
of government in line with our respective legal and institutional frameworks. 

Building more equitable
societies through education and training

WE RECOGNISE
 the unique
potential of education starting from early childhood to enable social mobility,
reduce inequalities, value diversity and build strong foundations for
equitable, inclusive, sustainable democratic societies.

WE ARE COMMITTED
 to
empowering all learners, with a focus on the most vulnerable in our societies,
to develop the knowledge, skills, attitudes and values to fulfil their
potential and contribute to the economic and social well-being of their
societies as they face constant change and sudden disruptions such as
technological advancements or the transition to a green economy. 

WE REAFFIRM
 our
commitment to innovative and evidence-based policies and investments that
support all learners and educators to actively engage in lifelong learning.

WE CALL
 on the
OECD, through the EDPC, and in collaboration with other relevant OECD
committees, to:

●
              

Support
countries to strengthen economic and social outcomes through education and
skills, and leverage synergies with other policy fields to ensure that education
and training contribute to better and more equitable economic and labour-market
outcomes, greater social participation, better-functioning democracies and a
more sustainable environment. 

●
              

Support
countries to ensure high quality teaching as a significant factor in improving
outcomes for learners, especially for those from disadvantaged backgrounds.

●
              

Strengthen
the evidence base on links between skills development, thriving economies and
equitable and inclusive societies.

●
              

Support
countries, based on sound evidence, in designing and implementing education and
skills policies that recognise the diverse backgrounds and needs of learners,
and provide them with effective and equitable opportunities to develop and
maintain their potential, and contribute to society.

●
              

Focus
its work on disaggregated data and indicators on distributional aspects of
access, resourcing and quality of education and skills training, and their
effects on educational, economic and social outcomes.

●
              

Regularly
track progress on equity internationally and improve the comparability and
consistency of data.

Reimagining education

WE PLEDGE
 to use the
opportunity of the post-COVID recovery in education to create a bold vision for
the future of learning. 

WE ARE COMMITTED
 to
developing education systems that help every learner to reach their potential
by promoting a diversity of learning pathways and innovative learning
environments, and ensuring high quality teaching, including effective
professional development of educators throughout their careers. 

WE CALL
 on the
OECD, through the EDPC, and in collaboration with other relevant OECD committees
to support countries to:

●
              

Create
and realise a bolder vision on how to leverage spaces, time, technology and
human resources to provide more effective and inclusive learning opportunities,
based on sound evidence.

●
              

Give
learners a voice in what they learn, how they learn, where they learn and when
they learn.

●
              

Develop
a whole-child/person and whole-of-society approach to reimagine education that
is fit for the future. 

●
              

Enable
educational institutions to fulfil their role in support of the cognitive,
social and emotional development of learners.

●
              

Develop
effective strategies to promote gender equality and support vulnerable groups
in education, including persons with disabilities.

●
              

Design
innovative learning environments and pedagogies based on sound evidence that
leverage the science of learning and the potential of digital technologies and
promote a responsible use of these tools.

●
              

Foster
open educational resources and strengthen interoperability of digital solutions
in ways that reconcile ethics, personal rights and privacy with the value of
big data and analytics in the field of education.

●
              

Identify
and explore emerging trends that could shape the future of education and
provide them with a platform for peer learning and collaboration in order to
learn from and with each other, and with other sectors.

●
              

Recognise
and address the challenges and pressures that stifle innovation and inhibit the
change required to achieve a bolder vision for education while maintaining the
commitment to education as foundational to fostering democratic and equitable
societies.

●
              

Recognise
and address the changing roles of education professionals and develop policies
that empower them.

●
              

Analyse
the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic on learners and how countries are
effectively addressing this in their respective education systems.

Supporting skills for
the future

WE SUPPORT
 learning
throughout the life course from birth through work and beyond.

WE ARE COMMITTED
 to helping
learners build skills for a digital, green, inclusive and democratic world that
balances the growth of individuals and the economic, social and environmental
well-being of societies.

WE CALL
 on the
OECD, through the EDPC, and in collaboration with other relevant OECD
committees to: 

●
              

Build
a new vision for skills development that keeps pace with changes in societies
and economies and supports the transition to net-zero.

●
              

Support
countries to foster environmental sustainability through education.

●
              

Support
countries in the development of strategies to empower and support all learners
appropriately to build foundational and transferable skills for current and
future labour markets as well as for digital and green democracies. 

●
              

Support
countries to collaborate with diverse social partners, communities, business,
and learners to anticipate future skills needs in constantly transforming
economies, societies and labour markets. 

●
              

Explore
approaches to measure the outcomes of education and training for all subsets of
the population, and strengthen the analysis of OECD surveys in a life course
perspective.

●
              

Support
countries to better integrate the world of learning with the world of work in
education, training, upskilling and reskilling.
